channel.title: Secure Boot Support
channel.description: Only makes sense when it doesnt depend on microsoft keys.

item.title: Secure Boot Support
item.dc:creator: Patrick
item.description:
github.com/dkms-project/dkms

Add configuration option for enabling and disabling module signing

main ← ArrayBolt3:arraybolt3/try_sign_modules
opened 05:00AM - 27 Jan 26 UTC
ArrayBolt3
+138 -1

In some situations (such as when building a redistributable system image), it is… desirable to suppress module signing and the generation of a Machine Owner Key, even if the system supports module signing. Introduce a new configuration option, "try_sign_modules", which allows one to explicitly enable or disable the module signing system. As publicly redistributable images are often built in a chroot, and should virtually never have a MOK generated at image build time, disable module signing by default when running in a chroot. Fixes: https://github.com/dkms-project/dkms/issues/574 (Note: This is entirely untested so far, I intend on testing it manually and am hoping that submitting this as a PR will trigger the CI to test it. My experience with Github Actions is lacking...)

Read full topic

item.title: Secure Boot Support
item.dc:creator: Patrick
item.description:
related:

github.com/dkms-project/dkms

DKMS should not generate a MOK by default when running inside a chroot

opened 05:22AM - 23 Jan 26 UTC
ArrayBolt3
enhancement help wanted

Some operating systems include DKMS and DKMS modules as part of their installabl…e OS image. DKMS builds these modules as part of the image build process in this scenario, but it also generates a Machine Owner Key for signing the modules it builds for use with Secure Boot. This means that a MOK ends up built into the actual OS image itself. If the OS image is then made public, the keys are made public. Anyone who wants to enroll a MOK into their system is almost certainly going to enroll that public MOK because they'll assume it was generated on their device since that's what DKMS usually does. Thus you can end up with users who have basically backdoored their system's Secure Boot mechanism. I would argue DKMS really shouldn't be creating a MOK at all when running in a chroot. If DKMS isn't running "directly on" the underlying OS, there's a very good chance it's being used to create an image that is going to be duplicated and deployed multiple times, and there is no good way to know if the MOK is useful or dangerous in those situations.

Read full topic

item.title: Secure Boot Support
item.dc:creator: Patrick
item.description:
The development goal of compatibility with Microsoft Secure Boot has already been accomplished.

Quote:

Kicksecure – 19 Dec 25

Secure Boot

Information on Secure Boot and DKMS Signing Key (MOK Key) Enrollment to ensure kernel modules can be loaded on systems with Secure Boot enabled.

Kicksecure Secure Boot Compatibility

Platform specific.

  • Kicksecure: Kicksecure is compatible with computer hardware provider default (Microsoft) provided keys. Disabling Secure Boot is optional. However, if the user keeps Secure Boot enabled, DKMS key enrollment is recommended, which is documented below.
  • Kicksecure for Qubes: See Qubes Specific.

This is a usability feature. It follows usability principle “reduce cognitive load”. Some article that I just found on the topic that summarizes the principle for reference what I mean by that: Design Principles for Reducing Cognitive Load | Marvel Blog - Marvel Blog

This is to reduce the challenges of for a first time Linux user to avoid having to guide them to go into their EFI settings to disable Secure Boot, which can be confusing, overwhelming and a blocker to Linux adoption.

Therefore no policy changes planned.

Secure Boot / full Verified Boot - without Microsoft keys - is also in development. Reference:

Verified Boot - Development Discussion Development
At the time of writing, verified boot with reasonable security and usability using user-custom keys for Open Source Linux desktop distributions comparable to some Android hardware is unavailable due to a lack of hardware/firmware support (at least on the Intel/AMD64 platform). The gigantic verified boot wiki page has grown as large as it has because @arraybolt3 and I have been trying to wrap our heads around how verified boot with reasonable security, usability, and user-custom keys for Open So…

Read full topic
